title: Decision on the merits: Sindacato Autonomo Comitato Nazionale Pompieri (CO.NA.PO.) v. Italy, Complaints No. 214/2022 and No. 215/2022
text: - 19 - European Committee of Social Rights Comité européen des Droits sociaux DECISION ON THE MERITS Adoption: 10 September 2025 Notification: 15 October 2025 Publication: 16 February 2026 Sindacato Autonomo Comitato Nazionale Pompieri (CO.NA.PO.) v. Italy Complaints No. 214/2022 and No. 215/2022 The European Committee of Social Rights, committee of independent experts established under Article 25 of the European Social Charter (“the Committee”), during its 350th session, attended by: Aoife NOLAN, President Tatiana PUIU, Vice-President George THEODOSIS, Vice-President Kristine DUPATE, General Rapporteur Karin Møhl LARSEN Yusuf BALCI Mario VINKOVIĆ Miriam KULLMANN Carmen SALCEDO BELTRÁN Franz MARHOLD Alla FEDOROVA Grega STRBAN Olivier DE SCHUTTER Kristina KOLDINSKÁ Carmen Constantina NENU Assisted by Henrik KRISTENSEN, Executive Secretary Having deliberated on 2 July and 10 September 2025 On the basis of the report presented by Tatiana PUIU, Delivers the following decision adopted on the latter date: PROCEDURE 1. The complaints lodged by the Sindacato Autonomo Comitato Nazionale Pompieri (CO.NA.PO.) v. Italy were both registered on 15 September 2022. 2. CO.NA.PO. alleges that certain measures adopted by the Government under Decree-Law No. 76/2020, in application of Law No. 183/2010, which aimed to reform labour market regulations and employment conditions, violate the rights of two groups of firefighters (Corpo Nazionale dei vigili del fuoco), namely those recruited before 1 January 2020 and currently in active service, and those who retired before 1 January 2020. CO.NA.PO. alleges violation of Articles 2§4 (the right to just conditions of work), 4§1 and 2 (the right to a fair remuneration), 12 (the right to social security), and Article E (non-discrimination) read in conjunction with these provisions of the revised European Social Charter (“the Charter”). 3. The first complaint relates to remuneration and pension regime applicable to firefighters currently in service. CO.NA.PO. alleges a violation of the above-mentioned Charter provisions due to the delay in the implementation of Law No. 183/2010, having resulted in discriminatory treatment between firefighters recruited before and after 1 January 2020, as well as between the firefighters and other personnel in Civil Protection Corps, as regards remuneration for work of equal value. 4. The second complaint concerns firefighters who retired between 2010 and 2020. CO.NA.PO. alleges a violation of the above-mentioned Charter provisions due to the delay in the implementation of Law No. 183/2010, as regards the equality in pension entitlement of firefighters who retired before 1 January 2020 and their colleagues still in service after 1 January 2020. 5. Given the related nature of the two complaints Nos. 214/2022 and 215/2022, pursuant to Rule 26A of the Rules, on 23 May 2023 the Committee ordered their joinder with a view to examining their admissibility. On 4 July 2023, the Committee declared the complaints admissible. 6. In accordance with Article 7 of the 1995 Protocol providing for a system of collective complaints (“the Protocol”), the Committee invited the Government to make written submissions on the merits of the complaint by 15 September 2023. 7. Referring to Article 7§§1 and 2 of the Protocol, the Committee invited the States Parties to the Protocol and the States having made a declaration in accordance with Article D§2 of the Charter as well as the international organisations of employers or workers mentioned in Article 27§2 of the Charter, to notify any observations they may wish to make on the complaints by 15 September 2023. 8. The Government requested and was granted a first extension of the deadline until 15 October 2023, which was subsequently postponed until 1 December 2023, to present its submissions on the merits. The Government’s submissions on the merits of the complaints were registered on 1 December 2023. 9. Pursuant to Rule 31§2 of the Rules, the President of the Committee invited CO.NA.PO. to submit a response to the Government’s submissions on the merits by 29 February 2024. CO.NA.PO.’s response was registered on 29 February 2024. 10. Pursuant to Rule 31§3 of the Rules, the Government was invited to submit a reply to CO.NA.PO.’s response by 15 May 2024. The Government’s reply was registered on 15 May 2024. SUBMISSIONS OF THE PARTIES A – The complainant organisation 11. According to CO.NA.PO., Decree-Law No. 76/2020, which in application of Law No. 183/2010 of 4 November 2010, guarantees equal treatment in respect of salary and pensions between the National Firefighters Corps and other personnel in Civil Protection Corps performing equal work, applies only to firefighters recruited as of 1 January 2020. No provision has been made for firefighters who were recruited or retired before that date. CO.NA.PO alleges that the delay in allocating funds for the revaluation of firefighters' salaries and pensions as provided for by Law No. 183/2010 and the failure to take into account service performed by firefighters who were recruited or retired before 2020 constitute: - a violation of the right to fair remuneration and an increased rate of remuneration for overtime work (Article 4§§1 and 2 of the Charter); - a violation of the right to protection in respect of inherently dangerous or unhealthy occupations (Article 2§4 of the Charter); - a violation of the obligation to raise the system of social security to a higher level (Article 12§3 of the Charter); - a violation of the principle of non-discrimination (Article E of the Charter). B – The respondent Government 12. The Government rejects the allegations of CO.NA.PO. and asks the Committee to find that there is no violation of the Charter provisions invoked. RELEVANT DOMESTIC LAW AND PRACTICE A – Constitutional principles 13. Constitution of Italy FUNDAMENTAL PRINCIPLES Article 3: “All citizens possess equal social dignity and are equal before the law, without distinction of sex, race, language, religion and political orientation, personal and social conditions.” PART I. RIGHTS AND DUTIES OF CITIZENS, TITLE II: ETHICAL AND SOCIAL RELATIONS Article 36: “Workers have the right to a remuneration proportional to the quantity and quality of their work and, in any case, sufficient to ensuring them and their families a free and dignified existence.” B – Domestic legislation 14. Firefighters' Corps Law No. 1570 of 27 December 1941, as amended Article 8 “[…] when performing their duties, firefighters shall be regarded as public security officers and, when travelling for service-related purposes, shall enjoy the same benefits as those granted to public law enforcement officials”. “Officers and non-commissioned officers shall have the status of judicial police officers; senior firefighters and ordinary firefighters shall have the status of judicial police officers.” 15. Legislative Decree No. 217/2005 - Organisation of the personnel of the National Firefighters Corps - as amended Article 1(2) “National Firefighters’ Corps is a civil organisation, national and unarmed, responsible for technical rescue, fire prevention and firefighting, and ensuring the safety of people and property.” 16. Law No. 183/2010 Delegations to the Government regarding arduous work, reorganisation of entities, leaves, expectations and permits, social safety nets, employment services, employment incentives, apprenticeships, female employment, as well as measures against undeclared work and provisions regarding public work and labour disputes Article 19 “1. For the purposes of defining the regulations, careers and contents of the employment relationship and economic, pension and social security protection, the specificity of the role of the Armed Forces, the Police Forces and the National Fire Brigade Corps is recognized, as well as the legal status of the personnel belonging to them, depending on the peculiarity of the tasks, obligations and personal limitations, provided for by laws and regulations, for the functions of protection of democratic institutions and defense of internal and external order and security, as well as for the specific operational efficiency requirements and the related employment in arduous activities. 2. The implementing framework of the principles and guidelines referred to in paragraph 1 is defined with subsequent legislative provisions, with which the necessary financial resources are also allocated.” 17. Law No. 160 of 27 December 2019, State budget forecast for the financial year 2020 and multi-year budget for the three-year period 2020-which was later converted into law by Law No. 120 of 11 September 2020 (Finance Law), with Article 20 of Decree-Law No. 76/2020 of 16 July 2020 Article 1(133) “In order to adopt regulatory measures aimed at enhancing the National Fire Brigade Corps, also with a view to greater harmonization of the economic treatment with that of the personnel of the Police Forces, a special fund is established in the budget of the Ministry of the Interior with an endowment of 65 million euros in the year 2020, 120 million euros in the year 2021 and 165 million euros annually starting from the year 2022. Consequently, starting from the same year, the resources referred to in Article 1, paragraph 1328, first period, of Law No. 296 of 27 December 2006, entered in the budget of the Ministry of the Interior pursuant to Article 23, paragraph 1-bis, of Law No. 31 December 2009. 196, are reduced by 10 million euros per year.” 18. Decree-Law No. 76 of 16 July 2020, - Urgent measures for simplification and digital innovation Article 20 Provisions concerning the National Fire Brigade “1. Table C attached to Legislative Decree No. 217 of 13 October 2005 is replaced by Table C in Annex A to this Decree, which contains Annexes No. 1, No. 2 and No. 3, respectively regulating, as of 1 January 2020, 1 January 2021 and 1 January 2022, the measures of the salary scale, risk allowances and monthly allowances, the specificity allowance and the risk and position remuneration fixed quota of the personnel of the National Fire Brigade. 2. The salary effects resulting from the application of Table C referred to in paragraph 1 constitute economic improvements pursuant to Article 12, paragraph 5, of Legislative Decree No. 19 August 2016. 177, and Article 261 of Legislative Decree No. 217 of 13 October 2005. 3. In order to deal with unforeseeable and urgent service needs, connected to the urgent technical rescue activity and the other institutional activities of the National Fire Corps as well as the related operational training, the annual allocation of overtime hours provided for by Article 11 of Law No. 246 of 10 August 2000 and Article 8-ter of Legislative Decree No. 53 of 14 June 2019, converted with amendments by Law No. 8 August 2019. 77, is increased by 55,060 hours for the year 2021 and by 401,900 hours starting from the year 2022. 4. In order to enhance the effectiveness of the institutional services performed by the National Fire Brigade Corps as well as to rationalize the framework of the related accessory remuneration institutions, the administration fund for non-managerial and non-executive staff of the National Fire Brigade Corps is increased by 693,011 euros from 1 January 2020, by 3,772,440 euros from 1 January 2021, by 13,972,000 euros from 1 January 2022 […] 5. In order to harmonize the system of allowances due to personnel performing specialist functions of the National Fire e Corps with that of the personnel of the Police Forces, the resources referred to in Article 17-bis, paragraph 5, of Legislative Decree 29 May 2017, no. 97 are increased: a) for the aviation sector, by €1,161,399 for the year 2021 and by €3,871,331 starting from the year 2022; b) for the diving sector, by €400,153 for the year 2021 and by €1,333,843 starting from the year 2022; c) for the nautical sector, including personnel performing lagoon fire-fighting services, of 552,576 euros for the year 2021 and 1,841,920 euros starting from the year 2022. 6. In recognition of the commitment made in order to deal with the exceptional and growing needs of public rescue, personnel belonging to the role of fire fighters and to the role of team leaders and department heads, as well as personnel belonging to the corresponding qualifications of the special fire-fighting roles (AIB) to be exhausted and the roles of the specialist functions of the National Fire Fighter Corps who accrue in the year 2021 a seniority of effective service of at least 32 years in the aforementioned Corps, a one-off allowance of 300 euros is paid. The same personnel who accrue in the year 2022 a seniority of effective service of at least 32 years in the aforementioned Corps, a one-off allowance of €400 is paid. 7. In relation to the specificity of the managerial functions and responsibilities connected to the needs in the field of public rescue, in order to encourage the improvement of the efficiency of the related services, the fund for the risk and position and performance remuneration of the non-general level managerial staff of the National Fire Brigade Corp is increased: a) for the variable portion of the risk and position remuneration of €52,553 from 1 January 2021 and of €363,938 from 1 January 2022; b) for the performance-related remuneration of Euro 23,346 from 1 January 2021 and Euro 161,675 from 1 January 2022. 8. For the same purposes as in paragraph 7, the fund for the risk and position remuneration and performance-related remuneration of the general management staff of the National Fire Brigade Corps is increased: a) for the variable portion of the risk and position remuneration of Euro 14,494 from 1 January 2021 and Euro 100,371 from 1 January 2022; b) for the performance-related remuneration of EUR 4,659 from 1 January 2021 and EUR 32,267 from 1 January 2022. 9. In order to enhance the effectiveness of the institutional services performed by the National Fire Brigade, without prejudice to the provisions of Article 17-bis, paragraph 2, letter c), of Legislative Decree 29 May 2017, n. 97, the productivity fund for the management staff of the National Fire Brigade is increased by EUR 715,341 from 1 January 2021 and by EUR 3,390,243 from 1 January 2022, also for the financing of the expenditure related to the establishment of the organizational positions referred to in Articles 199 and 223 of Legislative Decree 13 October 2005, n. 217. 10. In order to harmonize the remuneration elements of the personnel belonging to the technical-operational roles of the National Fire Brigade Corps with that belonging to the Police Forces, starting from 1 January 2021 the increase in the risk allowance, established pursuant to Article 64, paragraph 4, of the Presidential Decree of 4 August 1990, no. 335, is reabsorbed into the new measures envisaged for the risk allowance and indicated in the relevant Table C referred to in paragraph 1. 11. For the same purposes referred to in paragraphs 4, 7, 8 and 9, the incentive funds for the personnel of the National Fire Brigade Corps are annually increased, starting from the year 2020, by the resources indicated in Annex B to this provision. 12. Article 14 of Legislative Decree No. 97 of 29 May 2017 shall be interpreted as meaning that Article 261 of the same Legislative Decree No. 217 of 13 October 2005 shall apply to personnel belonging to the Fiamme rosse fire brigade sports group and to the musical band of the National Fire Brigade Corps in service on 31 December 2017, on the occasion of the classifications referred to in Articles 124 and 129 of Legislative Decree No. 217 of 13 October 2005. 217. 13. In the event that the personnel of the National Fire Corps, following the application of this article, obtain, in the form of fixed and continuous allowances, including conventional increases, an economic treatment that is lower than that enjoyed for the same reason at the time of the aforementioned application, the excess is attributed in the form of a pensionable allowance ad personam to be reabsorbed with subsequent economic improvements. 14. The costs arising from the implementation of this article, equal to 65 million euros for the year 2020, 120 million euros for the year 2021 and 164.5 million euros starting from the year 2022, including indirect costs, defined pursuant to article 17, paragraph 7, of law 31 December 2009, no. 196 and equal to 3.161 million euros for the year 2020, 5.8 million euros for the year 2021 and 7.6 million euros starting from 2022, shall be covered through the corresponding use of the resources of the fund referred to in article 1, paragraph 133, of law 27 December 2019, no. 160, entered in the budget forecast of the Ministry of the Interior. With subsequent regulatory provisions, within the spending limit of 500 thousand euros starting from the year 2022, provision is made for the enhancement of the operational staff of the National Fire Brigade Corps also through new hiring methods referred to in Article 1, paragraph 138, of Law 27 December 2019, no. 160. 15. The legal and economic effects referred to in this article take effect from 1 January 2020 and for social security purposes, such increases have effect exclusively with reference to the contribution periods accrued starting from the same date. 16. The Minister of Economy and Finance is authorised to make, with his own decrees, the necessary budget variations.” 19. Legislative Decree No. 139/06 reorganisation of the provisions relating to the functions and tasks of the National Firefighter Corps, pursuant to Article 11 of Law No. 229 of 29 July 2003 Article 35(1)(d) “For the purposes of this Law and when performing their duties, members of the Firefighters’ Corps, including both permanent personnel and volunteers, shall have the status of public security officers, when travelling for service-related purposes, shall enjoy the same benefits as those granted to public law enforcement officials (…).” 20. Law No. 234 of 30 December 2021- State budget forecast for the financial year 2022 and multi-year budget for the three-year period 2022-2024. Paragraph 95 “In relation to the specificity of the personnel of the Armed Forces, the Police Forces and the National Fire Brigade, recognized pursuant to Article 19 of Law No. 183 of 4 November 2010, a fund with an allocation of 20 million euros for the year 2022, 40 million euros for the year 2023 and 60 million euros starting from the year 2024 is established in the budget of the Ministry of Economy and Finance.” Paragraph 96 “The fund referred to in paragraph 95 is intended for the adoption of regulatory measures aimed at the gradual equalization of the relevant social security system, through the introduction, within the institutions already provided for the same personnel, of measures: a) compensatory with respect to the effects arising from the liquidation of pension treatments for personnel in service on the day preceding the date of entry into force of the relevant regulatory measure; b) supplementary to the supplementary pension forms referred to in Article 26, paragraph 20, of Law 23 December 1998, no. 448, for personnel inducted into the roles of the Armed Forces, the Police Forces and the National Fire Department, as of the date of entry into force of the relevant regulatory measure.” Paragraph 97 “The resources referred to in paragraph 95 are used by ensuring that at least 50 percent are allocated to the purposes referred to in letter b) of the same paragraph.” Paragraph 98 “In the first application, the resources referred to in paragraph 100 are intended for the allocation to the personnel of the National Fire Brigade, upon termination of service and for the purposes of calculating the pension base and the payment of the severance pay, of increases equal to 2.50 percent each to be calculated on the last salary table, including increases for recognized infirmity dependent on cause of service, benefits for combatants and equivalent and personal allowances in enjoyment, in the number of one starting from 1 January 2022, two starting from 1 January 2023, three starting from 1 January 2024, five starting from 1 January 2027 and six starting from 1 January 2028, computed in accordance with Article 4 of the Legislative Decree of 30 April 1997, n. 165.or the purposes referred to in paragraph 98, the expenditure is authorised, within the budget forecast of the Ministry of Labour and Social Policies, to be allocated to transfers in support of social security management, of Euro 1,815,820 for the year 2022, of Euro 3,662,464 for the year 2023, of Euro 5,477,793 for the year 2024, of Euro 5,442,669 for the year 2025, of Euro 5,426,139 for the year 2026, of Euro 9,008,205 for the year 2027 and of Euro 10,798,474 starting from the year 2028 and, within the budget forecast of the Ministry of the Interior for the increased effective contribution burden to burden of the administration, of Euro 5,492,854 for the year 2022, of Euro 32,665,384 starting from the year 2028.” RELEVANT INTERNATIONAL MATERIAL A – Council of Europe 21. Recommendation No. R (91) 11 on the European Code of Social Security "1. The aim of this Code is to set forth the standards to be followed by the member states of the Council of Europe in their social security systems in order to ensure that they provide adequate and equitable social protection to all persons. 2. The Code provides standards for social security benefits and the administration of social security systems, emphasizing the need for adequate and equitable compensation and protection for workers and their families." B – European Union 22. The Charter of Fundamental Rights of the European Union (2000/C 364/01) Article 21 Non-discrimination “1. Any discrimination based on any ground such as sex, race, colour, ethnic or social origin, genetic features, language, religion or belief, political or any other opinion, membership of a national minority, property, birth, disability, age or sexual orientation shall be prohibited. 2. Within the scope of application of the Treaty establishing the European Community and of the Treaty on European Union, and without prejudice to the special provisions of those Treaties, any discrimination on grounds of nationality shall be prohibited.” Article 31 Fair and just working conditions “1. Every worker has the right to working conditions which respect his or her health, safety and dignity. 2. Every worker has the right to limitation of maximum working hours, to daily and weekly rest periods and to an annual period of paid leave.” 23. The Treaty on the Functioning of the European Union (TFEU) Article 9 “In defining and implementing its policies and activities, the Union shall take into account requirements linked to the promotion of a high level of employment, the guarantee of adequate social protection, the fight against social exclusion, and a high level of education, training and protection of human health. […]”. Article 151 “The Union and the Member States, having in mind fundamental social rights such as those set out in the European Social Charter signed at Turin on 18 October 1961 and in the 1989 Community Charter of the Fundamental Social Rights of Workers, shall have as their objectives the promotion of employment, improved living and working conditions, so as to make possible their harmonization while the improvement is being maintained, proper social protection, dialogue between management and labour, the development of human resources with a view to lasting high employment and the combating of exclusion […]”. 24. The European Pillar of Social Rights: Chapter II, Fair Working Conditions “5. Secure and adaptable employment “a. Regardless of the type and duration of the employment relationship, workers have the right to fair and equal treatment regarding working conditions, access to social protection and training. The transition towards open-ended forms of employment shall be fostered.” 6. Wages “a. Workers have the right to fair wages that provide for a decent standard of living. b. Adequate minimum wages shall be ensured, in a way that provide for the satisfaction of the needs of the worker and his/her family in the light of national economic and social conditions, whilst safeguarding access to employment and incentives to seek work. In-work poverty shall be prevented. c. All wages shall be set in a transparent and predictable way according to national practices and respecting the autonomy of the social partners.” C – United Nations 25. International Covenant on Economic, Social and Cultural Rights. Adopted by the General Assembly of the United Nations on 16 December 1966 Article 7 “The States Parties to the present Covenant recognize the right of everyone to the enjoyment of just and favourable conditions of work which ensure, in particular: (a) Remuneration which provides all workers, as a minimum, with: (i) Fair wages and equal remuneration for work of equal value without distinction of any kind, in particular women being guaranteed conditions of work not inferior to those enjoyed by men, with equal pay for equal work; (ii) A decent living for themselves and their families in accordance with the provisions of the present Covenant; (b) Safe and healthy working conditions […]”. Article 9 “The States Parties to the present Covenant recognise the right of everyone to social security, including social insurance” 26. Committee on Economic, Social and Cultural Rights (CESCR) CESCR General Comment No. 19: The right to social security (c) Old age 15. “States parties should take appropriate measures to establish social security schemes that provide benefits to older persons, starting at a specific age, to be prescribed by national law. The Committee stresses that States parties should establish a retirement age that is appropriate to national circumstances which take account of, inter alia, the nature of the occupation, in particular work in hazardous occupations and the working ability of older persons. States parties should, within the limits of available resources, provide non-contributory old-age benefits, social services and other assistance for all older persons who, when reaching the retirement age prescribed in national legislation, have not completed a qualifying period of contributions or are not otherwise entitled to an old-age insurance-based pension or other social security benefit or assistance, and have no other source of income.” CESCR General Comment No. 23 (2016): The right to just and favourable conditions of work (Art. 7 of the Covenant) : 11. “Not only should workers receive equal remuneration when they perform the same or similar jobs, but their remuneration should also be equal even when their work is completely different but nonetheless of equal value when assessed by objective criteria. This requirement goes beyond only wages or pay to include other payments or benefits paid directly or indirectly to workers […]. 12. “The extent to which equality is being achieved requires an ongoing objective evaluation of whether the work is of equal value and whether the remuneration received is equal […]. 13. “Objective job evaluation is important to avoid indirect discrimination when determining rates of remuneration and comparing the relative value of different jobs […]”. D – International Labour Organisation 27. Convention No.111 concerning Discrimination (Employment and Occupation) Article 2 “Each Member for which this Convention is in force undertakes to declare and pursue a national policy designed to promote, by methods appropriate to national conditions and practice, equality of opportunity and treatment in respect of employment and occupation, with a view to eliminating any discrimination in respect thereof.” THE LAW PRELIMINARY CONSIDERATIONS As to the provisions of the Charter at stake 28. In Complaint No. 214/2022 CO.NA.PO. alleges that the harmonisation of salary and pension conditions between National Firefighters Corps (Corpo Nazionale dei vigili del fuoco) and other personnel within Civil Protection Corps was only implemented belatedly in 2020 through Finance Act of 2020 and Decree-Law 76/2020. CO.NA.PO. highlights that this harmonisation measure applies only to personnel recruited on or after 1 January 2020, without any adjustment or compensation for firefighters hired before that date, who are still in service and constitute the majority of the workforce. 29. In particular, as regards salaries, CO.NA.PO. contends that the late implementation of the legislation (Article 19 of Law No. 183/2010) recognising the similarity of the legal status between the members of the National Firefighters Corps and other personnel in Civil Protection Corps, resulted in both “internal” discrimination within the National Firefighters Corps (between those recruited before and after 1 January 2020) and “external” discrimination in relation to other personnel in the Civil Protection corps with similar public service functions, such as Police. 30. As regards pensions, CO.NA.PO. submits that unequal treatment in salary entitlements also has repercussions on pension rights. Firefighters hired before 1 January 2020 not only continued to receive lower salaries and allowances than their colleagues recruited after that date, performing the same duties, but their future pensions will also be negatively affected. This is because the pensionable contribution base of their employment period prior to 1 January 2020 will be calculated under the previous (less advantageous) remuneration regime, with the carry-over effect of this part. 31. In Complaint No. 215/2022 CO.NA.PO. denounces the pension treatment of firefighters who retired between 2010 and 31 December 2019, alleging that it is less favourable than that accorded to fellow firefighters who retired after 1 January 2020. The firefighters who retired before 1 January 2020 are excluded from the allocation of funds introduced to implement the pension harmonisation, which only applies to retirees from 2020 onwards. CO.NA.PO. argues that, although these firefighters were covered by the harmonisation commitment made in 2010, they were prevented from its benefits, solely due to the delayed implementation of the reforms. By the time the new provisions entered into force, they had already retired and are therefore excluded from the scope of the updated regime. 32. In these complaints, CO.NA.PO alleges the violation of the following provisions of the Charter: - Article 2§4 (the right to just conditions of work)  - Article 4§1 and 2 (the right to a fair remuneration) and Article E (non-discrimination) of the Charter - Article 12 (the right to social security). 33. As regards the allegations concerning Article 4§§1 and 2 of the Charter, the Committee observes that the arguments of CO.NA.PO relate to the remuneration for equal work and equal treatment as regards supplementary benefits. Relying primarily on Article 4§1 alone and in conjunction with Article E of the Charter, CO.NA.PO alleges that the ensuing difference in treatment between firefighters hired before and after 1 January 2020 constitutes unlawful discrimination. 34. The Committee recalls that Article 4§1 of the Charter guarantees the right of workers to a fair remuneration such as to ensure a decent standard of living, ordinarily assessed in relation to the minimum wage in the State concerned. The Committee observes, however, that the complaints do not raise issues related to a decent standard of living for firefighters or other persons performing similar duties. It therefore considers that the allegations of discrimination such as those raised in the present complaints are more appropriately examined under Article 1§2 of the Charter, which entails, inter alia, the elimination of all forms of discrimination in employment, including with respect to remuneration. 35. The Committee recalls that it has the power to define and interpret the law to be applied to the facts of a complaint, including by examining it under provisions of the Charter that are different from those expressly relied upon by the complainant organisation (Union Syndicale Solidaires SDIS v. France Complaint No. 176/2019 and Union Syndicale Solidaires SDIS v. France Complaint No. 193/2020, decision on the merits of 12 September 2023, §86). 36. Accordingly, the Committee decides to examine the allegations in question under Article 1§2 of the Charter, while considering the allegations under Article 4§§1 to 2 of the Charter to be insufficiently substantiated. As regards the alleged violation of Article E (non-discrimination) read in conjunction with Articles 1§2 of the Charter, the Committee recalls that in matters relating to discrimination in employment, it is not necessary to combine the said Article with Article E, as Article 1§2 alone prohibits such discrimination (Fellesforbundet for Sjøfolk (FFFS) v. Norway, Complaint No. 74/2011, decision on the merits of 2 July 2013, § 51). 37. As regards the alleged violation of Article 12, CO.NA.PO. argues that the State has not ensured that social security systems are adapted to the situation and specific needs of the workers concerned, in order to guarantee equitable access to social benefits. Specifically, it alleges that pension adjustments reflecting newly ascertained risks were not implemented for firefighters during the period 2010-2020, nor for those who had already retired before Decree-Law No. 76/2020 came into force. CO.NA.PO. asserts that the Government should adopt measures to benefit the entire category of firefighters hired before 1 January 2020, either by introducing transitional provisions to equalise their wage and pension entitlements, or by providing compensatory relief by way of a lump sum. 38. The Committee recalls that while under Article 12§1 States Parties must ensure the existence of a social security system established by law and functioning in practice, Article 12§3 requires States Parties to improve their social security system and ensure that reforms that are carried out with a view to improving the social security system do not exclude entire categories of workers from the social protection offered by this system. The Committee considers that the issue at stake should be examined under Article 12§3. It further notes that CO.NA.PO. also alleges discriminatory treatment in relation to social security entitlements and thus decides to examine this aspect of the complaints under Article 12§3 and Article E read in conjunction with Article 12§3. 39. As regards the alleged violation of Article 2§4 of the Charter, the Committee observes that underlying arguments are closely related to those raised under Article 1§2 and 12, particularly concerning the allegedly discriminatory provision of additional benefits for performing strenuous work. Given this overlap, and the fact that core issues of remuneration and pensions protection and working conditions are already examined under Articles 1§2 and 12§3, the Committee considers that no separate issue arises under Article 2§4 of the Charter. 40. In light of these considerations, the Committee decides to examine the merits of the complaints under Articles 1§2, 12§3 and Article E read in conjunction with 12§3 of the Charter. I. ALLEGED VIOLATION OF ARTICLE 1§2 OF THE CHARTER 41. Article 1§2 of the Charter reads as follows: Article 1 – The right to work Part I: “Everyone shall have the opportunity to earn his living in an occupation freely entered upon.” Part II: “With a view to ensuring the effective exercise of the right to work, the Parties undertake: (…) 2. to protect effectively the right of the worker to earn his living in an occupation freely entered upon; (…)” A – Arguments of the parties 1. The complainant organisation 42. CO.NA.PO. alleges that a significant number of Italian firefighters in active service (at least 4,600) have not received equal treatment compared to personnel within Civil Protection Corps performing the equivalent duties, particularly in view of the risks associated with their work. According to CO.NA.PO., this situation constitutes a breach of international principles and standards prohibiting discrimination in the provision of fair pay, compensation for hazardous and strenuous work, and access to an adequate and progressively improving pension and social security system under equivalent social and employment conditions. 43. CO.NA.PO. refers to Law No. 183/2010 adopted to regulate in a similar manner all strenuous public services by providing for the reorganisation of entities, time off work, leaves of absence and permitted absences, employment services, incentives to work, apprenticeships and female employment. According to Article 19 of this Law, when establishing the regulatory frameworks, careers and content of the employment relationship as well as financial, pension and welfare protection, the specific nature of the role performed by the Armed forces, the Police forces and the National Firefighters Corps as well as the legal status of the respective personnel should be recognised, having regard to the specific nature of the tasks, obligations and personal constraints provided for under the law and regulations with regard to the functions of protecting democratic institutions and defending public order and internal and external security, as well as operational efficiency required and the related deployment to strenuous activities. The Law No.183/2010 established a specific emolument known as the “special duties allowance”, recognising for the first time the equivalence in terms of status among the Armed forces, Police forces and the National Firefighters’ Corps, all of which are exposed to the same risks and strain. According to CO.NA.PO. this created a legitimate expectation of salary and pension harmonisation for all members of the various forces and corps in service from that time onwards. 44. CO.NA.PO. submits that the Law No.183/2010 represented the first stage in a harmonisation process, amounting to both legal and policy provision by the State to align the salaries and pensions of all workers to achieve fair remuneration for all strenuous services. 45. However, according to CO.NA.PO., the Government only implemented these measures ten years later with Article 20 of Decree-Law No. 76/2020 of 16 July 2020, converted into law by Law No. 120 of 11 September 2020, with effect from 2020 onwards and without any transitional provisions. As a result, equal treatment in terms of salary and pension for firefighters was thus ensured as compared with the members of police forces falling under the Civil Protection Corps, all of whom are exposed to the same risks and strenuous work for the purposes of Law No. 183/2010. 46. CO.NA.PO. claims that the pension and salary recognition, which was introduced with a 10-year delay in implementing the 2010 legislation, was applied only to firefighters recruited as of 1 January 2020. No financial provision was made for employment relationships established prior to that date, even though the majority of firefighters remained in active service under those earlier contracts. 47. According to CO.NA.PO. the alleged discrimination results from the failure to apply and implement legislation which, considered in the abstract, would by contrast have represented an important step in the recognition of the strenuous and risky work of all firefighters when compared with all other personnel in Civil Protection Corps that are subject to the same operational risks and that are required to perform strenuous work. 48. In this context, CO.NA.PO. alleges the following: - “external” discrimination between National Firefighters Corps and other personnel in Civil Protection Corps performing identical duties in respect of the period 2010-2020 for which no adjustments were made for the former; - “internal” discrimination within the National Firefighters Corps itself, between personnel still in service whose contract started before 1 January 2020 and thus straddles the period falling before and after the application of the new, economically more favourable provisions (from 2020 onwards) and those who were recruited on or after 1 January 2020 and perform the same duties under a more favourable regime. 49. CO.NA.PO. argues that all members of the National Firefighters’ Corps who have worked under the terms introduced by Law No. 183/2010 have a legitimate expectation of equal treatment. The absence of a transitional regime for those recruited before 1 January 2020, despite their continuous service since the adoption of the law, constitutes a loophole that has resulted in discrimination, particularly in relation to the realisation of this legitimate economic expectation of harmonised remuneration and pension entitlements. 50. In its submissions CO.NA.PO. provides an example of the differences in remuneration in the years 2018 and 2019 between the co-ordinator firefighter and a senior corporal, police officer, whose work is said to be of equal value. Assuming a length of service of between 17 and 21 years, the co-ordinator firefighter received a monthly salary of € 1 677.33 whereas a senior corporal, with the same length of service received € 1 802.75, resulting in a difference of € 125.42 per month or, when multiplied by 13 months, the annual difference of € 1 630.54. As regards the risk allowance for the same positions, the firefighter received € 518.54 whereas the police officer received € 662.88, resulting in a monthly difference of € 144.34 (or € 1 876.42 over 13 months). As regards the special duties allowance, the firefighter received € 72.87 whereas the police officer received a “functional allowance” of € 121.53, resulting in a difference of € 48.66 (or € 632.58 over 13 months). Thus, the total difference in remuneration between that received by the co-ordinator firefighter and a senior corporal police officer was € 1 630.54 + 1 876.42 + 632.58 = € 4 139.54. 51. In conclusion, CO.NA.PO. states that on the one hand, the concept of harmonisation and salary and pension equivalence was introduced based on analogy and equivalence between corps and duties. Consequently, all firefighters are deemed to be equivalent with other corps in terms of risks and strenuous work and thus should benefit from salary and pension allowances in the same manner as other corps and forces. On the other hand, however, the action taken by the legislature and by the public administration has been incomplete and discriminatory, as these principles of equality and salary and pension adjustment have only been implemented after a delay of ten years. Furthermore, when provision was made for the delayed entry into force of this equivalence, an entirely arbitrary date was chosen for it to start applying (1 January 2020), and no provision was made for any transitional adjustment. As a result, an entire class of workers whose circumstances are identical and who, despite having aspired to the promise of equal treatment since 2010, have thus been excluded from the benefits of the salary and pension adjustment on the sole basis of having been recruited before the late implementation of the legislation enacted in 2010. As a result, they will receive a permanently lower salary today, and a lower pension in future. 2. The respondent Government 52. The Government refutes the claim that the salary improvements introduced by Decree-Law No. 76/2020 are only applicable to firefighters recruited after 2020. It asserts that the salary harmonisation measures were applicable to all personnel in service as of January 1, 2020, ensuring that all firefighters benefit from these increases. Thus, the Government maintains that no violation of Article E has occurred, as it has acted within its margin of appreciation in addressing these issues. 53. The Government argues that the allegation of discrimination is unfounded, asserting that all measures taken in implementing the legislative provisions aim to achieve equal treatment for all firefighters, regardless of their recruitment date. The Government emphasises that the legislative framework provides for the necessary steps to ensure equity among all personnel. 54. Moreover, the Government argues that the legislative framework established under Article 19 of Law No. 183/2010 provided a programmatic basis for the conditions of employment for firefighters. This framework allowed for the development of subsequent legislative measures, which have been enacted to address the remuneration and working conditions of the personnel within the National Firefighters Corps. The Government contends that the provision in question had a clear programmatic nature and did not contain any precept as to the deadline within which the legislature had to adopt the legislative measures indicated therein and which, as mentioned above, would also identify and make available the necessary financial resources. 55. By Law No. 160/2019, Article 1, paragraph 133, resources were allocated as of the year 2020 for the adoption of regulatory provisions aimed at enhancing the National Firefighters Corps, also with a view to greater harmonisation of its economic treatment with that of Police force personnel. This objective was implemented through Article 20 of Decree-Law No. 76/2020, converted into Law No. 120/2020, which harmonised the salary elements. 56. The Government states that the salary harmonisation introduced by Decree-Law No. 76/2020 was applied as of 1 January 2020. It contends that CO.NA.PO.’s allegation fails to consider that Article 19(2) of Law No. 183/2010 expressly referred to subsequent legislative measures, which would also allocate the necessary financial resources to implement the harmonisation measures. 57. The Government states that Law No. 183/2010 introduced a programmatic provision, leaving broad discretion to the legislature as to both the timing and content of the subsequent legislative intervention. The principle that members of the National Firefighters Corps were to be placed on an equal footing (with harmonisation of the relevant disciplines) with members of the other armed forces, and such programmatic provisions do not confer individual entitlements and cannot bind subsequent legislatures. 58. According to the Government, when faced with a regulation of a programmatic nature it is left up to the legislator’s free choice whether not to implement or abrogate the regulation. Moreover, a regulation of a programmatic nature does not have the force to bind the subsequent legislator, whose choices remain fully discretionary. In the system of the sources of Italian law, in fact, a law that only provides for future regulatory intervention by the ordinary legislature is not binding on the latter, since they are sources of equal rank, and the legislature may subsequently decide to a) abrogate that previous law; b) not implement it (which is equivalent to tacit abrogation); c) implement it only when, within the scope of its own discretionary policy choices, it deems it appropriate to do so. 59. The Government states that even before 2020, a previous pay enhancement intervention had been carried out as a result of Article 15 of Decree-Law No. 97/2017. The provision established a fund in order to enhance the special conditions of professional employment of the National Firefighters Corps as a result of the reorganisation and applied by virtue of Presidential Decrees No. 47 and No. 48/2018, with the establishment of a so-called specificity allowance on account of seniority role and related responsibilities. 60. The Government further refers to Article 20 of Decree-Law No. 76/2020, according to which the cases in which the firefighters, following the application of this Article, receive, by way of fixed and continuous allowances, including the conventional steps, an economic treatment inferior to that in enjoyment for the same title at the time of the aforementioned application, the surplus shall be allocated in the form of a pensionable and personal allowance to be reabsorbed with the subsequent economic improvements. 61. The Government further contends that with specific reference to the path of harmonisation for the personnel of the public rescue and security-defence sectors, the 2022 Finance Law intervened under the social security profile. The law established the gradual extension to the National Firefighters Corps, from the year 2022 and until the completion of the alignment, of the institution of periodic salary increases (so-called six steps), already in force for the calculation of the retirement and end-of-service benefits of the police forces. 62. In conclusion, the Government states that the complaint should be declared unfounded, because: - Article 19 of Law No. 183/2010 has only programmatic content and did not impose binding deadlines on the legislature; - the Italian State has in any case taken steps to introduce provisions and institutions aimed at improving the economic and social security treatment of the employees of the firefighter’s corps, including salary harmonisation and social security adjustments 63. The Government further rejects CO.NA.PO.‘s allegation that the economic improvements brought about by the legislative measure are only applicable to firefighters recruited after the year 2020 (the year in which the new salary measures took effect). On this point, the Government contends that contrary to what CO.NA.PO. asserts, the situation is completely different, since it is self-evident that the salary improvements in question concerned all staff in service on 1 January 2020. In fact, the workers hired before 2020 had their salaries recalculated as of 2020 but not for the period before, in accordance with the harmonisation measure, while those hired after 2020 were given the “harmonised” salary due. 64. Therefore, the Government maintains that no discrimination occurred between personnel hired before and after 2020. The salary improvements brought about, as from 2020, by the aforementioned Article 20 of Decree-Law No. 76/2020, could not, however, be applied to firefighters who had left as of 31 December 2019 and who, therefore, did not benefit from them, including in terms of their pensions. 65. On this last aspect, the Government further indicates that the financing brought about by Article 1, paragraph 133, of Law No. 160/2019, destined for the salary harmonisation of the National Firefighters Corps’ personnel, started from the year 2020 (with €65 million for the first year, €120 million for 2021 and €165 million at regime from 2022). 66. The provision in question, financed as of the year 2020, provided for the new salary for the future, for all those in service on the planned effective date. Therefore, the argument that the legal provision (again, Decree-Law No. 76/2020) should have also applied to persons who retired before the year 2020 cannot be accepted, as this would be an inadmissible interpretation conferring retroactive effect on the rule. B – Assessment of the Committee 67. The Committee recalls that Article 1§2 of the Charter requires States Parties to protect effectively the right of workers to earn their living in an occupation freely entered upon (Syndicat national des professions du tourisme v. France, Complaint No. 6/1999, decision on the merits of 10 October 2000, §24). This obligation requires inter alia the elimination of all forms of discrimination (direct or indirect) in employment, whatever the legal nature of the professional relationship (Conclusions XVI-1 (2002), Iceland). Discriminatory acts prohibited by Article 1§2 are those that may occur in connection with employment conditions in general (in particular with regard to remuneration, training, promotion, transfer and dismissal or other detrimental action) (Conclusions XVI-1, 2002, Austria). 68. The Committee further recalls that according to Article 1§2, discrimination may arise either by treating people in the same situation differently or by treating people in different situations identically (International Federation of Human Rights (FIDH) v. Belgium, Complaint No. 62/2010, decision on the merits of 21 March 2012, §49). Discrimination is defined as a difference in treatment between persons in comparable situations where it does not pursue a legitimate aim, is not based on objective and reasonable grounds or is not proportionate to the aim pursued (Syndicat national des professions du tourisme v. France, Complaint No.6/1999 decision on the merits of 10 October 2000, §§24-25). 69. In the present case, the Committee’s assessment of the alleged discriminatory treatment concerns two comparator groups: (i) firefighters in the National Firefighters Corps compared to Police officers in the Civil Protection Corps (the alleged “external” discrimination); and (ii) firefighters recruited before and after 1 January 2020 (the alleged “internal” discrimination). Firefighters and Police officers – the alleged “external discrimination” 70. The Committee notes that according to Article 19 of Law No.183/2010, for the purposes of defining the regulations, careers and contents of the employment relationship and economic, pension and social security protection, the special role of the Armed Forces, the Police Forces and the National Firefighters Corps has been recognised, as well as the legal status of the personnel belonging to them, particularly in relation to operational efficiency requirements for employment in arduous activities. 71. The Committee notes that the adoption of this law was a culmination of the long process that led to the recognition of the need to improve and harmonise the working conditions and remuneration of the personnel within the Civil Protection Corps, by recognising the right of firefighters to equal pay for equal work in comparison to their counterparts in other functional categories, such as police force. 72. In this context, the Committee considers that, with the entry into force of Decree-Law 76/2020 and the allocation of resources as of 2020 for the adoption of regulatory provisions aimed at enhancing the National Firefighters Corps, including with a view to the gradual harmonisation of its economic treatment with that of police force personnel, the unequal treatment in respect of salaries and supplementary allowances appears to have been brought to an end for all staff in service as of 1 January 2020. 73. The Committee takes note of the complainant organisation’s argument that the delayed implementation of the legislation envisaging wage equalisation raises concerns. In the complainant organisation’s view, the core legal issue is not merely the non-retroactivity of salary increases, but whether the failure to recognise and remedy the inequality during the transitional period amounts to unjustified discrimination the Charter. 74. The Committee notes the Government’s submission that although Law No. 183/2010 expressed an intention to increase wages and allowances, it was of a programmatic nature and required further legislative and financial enactments to become effective. It further notes that the subsequent adoption of Decree-Law No. 76/2020, which introduced salary alignment, reflects a legislative and administrative acknowledgment of the equivalence of roles performed by firefighters and police personnel within the Civil Protection Corps and constitutes a concrete step towards the realisation of the objectives set out in the 2010 law. 75. The Committee observes that the difference in remuneration between the two groups prior to 1 January 2020 was addressed through legislative measures that introduced a harmonised salary regime applicable to all firefighters in service from that date. While the transitional period resulted in less favourable treatment for firefighters in service before 2020, the Committee considers that the arrangements pursued a legitimate aim, were based on objective and reasonable grounds, and were implemented through a structured legislative process that led to the removal of the differential treatment. It further considers that the decision to implement the equalisation prospectively, without retroactive compensation, was a policy choice within the State’s margin of appreciation. The absence of retroactive financial adjustment does not constitute discrimination within the meaning of Article 1§2 of the Charter, as the measures adopted brought the unequal treatment to an end and were applied uniformly from the date of their entry into force. Therefore, the Committee considers that the situation does not amount to “external” discrimination. 76. In the light of the above, the Committee holds that there is no violation of Article 1§2 of the Charter. Firefighters recruited before and after 1 January 2020 - the alleged “internal discrimination” 77. The Committee notes CO.NA.PO.’s allegation that the non-application of Law 183/2010 during the period 2010 – 2020 resulted in differential treatment between firefighters recruited before and after 1 January 2020. CO.NA.PO. argues that Decree-Law No. 76/2020, while introducing a harmonised salary system applicable from that date, did not include any transitional or compensatory measures for firefighters who had already been in service during the preceding ten years. According to the complainant organisation, this has led to internal inequality within the National Firefighters Corps as those recruited after 1 January 2020 benefit from improved financial conditions without having experienced the earlier period of lower remuneration. 78. The Committee notes that the remuneration and pension provisions introduced by Decree-Law No. 76/2020 have been applied uniformly to all firefighters in service as of 1 January 2020. It observes however that this legislation, while applying to all firefighters in service as of 1 January 2020, did not provide for retroactive adjustment for firefighters recruited before that date. In this respect, CO.NA.PO. requests that the State adopt positive measures to compensate for the pecuniary losses incurred by this group during the period 2010-2020. 79. The Committee notes that, as of 1 January 2020, the equal pay scheme established by Decree-Law No. 76/2020 has been applied uniformly to all firefighters in service, irrespective of their date of recruitment. It also takes note of CO.NA.PO.'s submission concerning the legitimate expectation created by Law No. 183/2010. While acknowledging that the absence of transitional or compensatory measures may have placed firefighters recruited prior to that date at a disadvantage as compared to those recruited after 2020, the Committee considers that the non-application of improved employment conditions retroactively in the instant case does not amount to a violation of the Charter, insofar as the subsequent reforms have been implemented in a non-discriminatory manner. The Committee considers that the legislative decision to apply the harmonised regime prospectively constitutes a general policy measure pursuing a legitimate aim and based on objective and reasonable grounds. Therefore, the Committee considers that the situation does not amount to internal discrimination. 80. Consequently, the Committee holds that there is no violation of Article 1§2 of the Charter. II. ALLEGED VIOLATION OF ARTICLE 12§3 OF THE CHARTER OR ARTICLE E READ IN CONJUNCTION WITH ARTICLE 12§3 OF THE CHARTER 81. Articles 12§3 and E read as follow: Article 12 – The right to social security Part I: “All workers and their dependents have the right to social security.” Part II: “With a view to ensuring the effective exercise of the right to social security, the Parties undertake: (…) 3. to endeavour to raise progressively the system of social security to a higher level; (…)” Article E – Non-discrimination “The enjoyment of the rights set forth in this Charter shall be secured without discrimination on any ground such as race, colour, sex, language, religion, political or other opinion, national extraction or social origin, health, association with a national minority, birth or other status.” A – Arguments of the parties 1. The complainant organisation 82. CO.NA.PO. argues that the disparity in pension benefits between firefighters recruited before and after January 1, 2020, constitutes a violation of Article 12 of the Charter, which guarantees the right to social security. Firefighters recruited after this date are entitled to more favorable pension schemes, even though all firefighters perform the same duties and responsibilities. The complainant submits that this discrepancy in social security provisions undermines the right to equal treatment in access to pension entitlements. 83. It further asserts that the Government’s failure to align the pension benefits of firefighters hired before 2020 with those recruited as of 1 January 2020 results in unequal access to social security protection. Firefighters recruited prior to 1 January 2020 are not only paid lower salaries and allowances but also face disadvantageous pension conditions compared to colleagues with the same or less seniority hired under the new scheme. 84. In particular, CO.NA.PO. alleges that the lower and less favourable salary basis (and thus the lower basis for calculating pension rights and obligations) accruing until 31 December 2019 will have a knock-on effect over the coming years thereby giving rise to a discriminatory and less favourable pension regime. This regime exists at the same time as and in parallel with the regime applicable to their colleagues working alongside them, who were hired on or after 1 January 2020, who have had the same legitimate expectation established in 2010, of receiving a full salary and pension, and who perform the same duties. 85. CO.NA.PO. maintains that the retirement conditions applicable to firefighters who retired before and after 1 January 2020 are entirely identical in terms of the expectations of the two groups, given that all were in active service when Law No. 183/2010 entered into force. In fact, the law makes no distinction between the two categories; the issue relates to the less favourable previous departure conditions applied to those who retired earlier. The calculation of their entitlements results in an unlimited deferral (trascinamento), which, in practice leads to the coexistence, at the same time, of different and more advantageous treatment for their colleagues for the purposes of contributions to their future pension. According to CO.NA.PO., retirement pensions paid to employees performing the same duties cannot therefore be different solely on the grounds of the late allocation of the relevant funds attributable to legislative inaction. Such a discrepancy arises from a random time factor, unrelated to any foreseeable or objectively justified parameter, and is therefore not based on any objective or reasonable criterion. 86. CO.NA.PO also highlights that approximately 350 firefighters retired before 2020 without any pension adjustments to place them on an equal footing with other personnel. It asserts that the non-applicability of the new pension adjustments to the pensions of individuals who nonetheless ran them up to 2020, but retired before the 2020 legislation came into force, constitutes a failure to make pension adjustments in their case, resulting in irreparable damage due to the delay in allocating the funds and in achieving the promised harmonisation. 87. Referring to Article 12§3 of the Charter, CO.NA.PO. recalls the State’s obligation to adapt regularly social security systems according to newly ascertained risks. If the national legislation does not provide for adjustment, extension or development according to the assessed risks, the Committee in its jurisprudence has tolerated this effect only if: a) it was a provisional period, or b) if the non-adaptation was aimed at maintaining a certain system of social security and social insurance (Statement of interpretation on Article 12§3, Conclusions XIII-4) and in any case c) it did not prevent members of the society from continuing to enjoy effective protection against the assessed risks (Finnish Society of Social Rights v. Finland, Complaint No. 172/2018, decision on the merits of 14 September 2022, §69). 88. CO.NA.PO. asserts that in the present case, all of these three conditions permitting pension non-adjustment are lacking, namely because a) the discriminatory effect of the non-adjustment of the risk allowances is not provisional but will produce continuous strain in the current employment treatment and in the future pension; b) this sectoral non-adjustment is not aimed at maintaining a previous system, on the contrary, it shows itself as an oversight or shortcoming of a single category, in the opposite framework of an innovative reform of the system itself; c) the results obtained precisely with the 2020 legislation demonstrate the intolerability of the previous system, which still remained in force for those hired before 2020. 89. CO.NA.PO. argues that there are no objective and reasonable grounds for maintaining this gap that is causing the difference in treatment. Regardless of the programmatic nature of the 2010 legislation, it argues that all firefighters, including those who retired prior to 2020, had a legitimate expectation that inequalities would be addressed and harmonisation achieved. 90. It further argues that the 2020 legislation was introduced precisely to implement and enact the 2010 legislation. Therefore, excluding firefighters from the scope of this implementation on the basis of the timing of their retirement is inconsistent and unjustified. 91. In this context, CO.NA.PO. asks the Committee to assess the shortcomings of the Italian legal system, particularly the failure to implement the pension harmonisation measures effectively and without discrimination, which it considers incompatible with the requirements of the European Social Charter. 2. The respondent Government 92. In its response, the Government states that the pension schemes and benefits for firefighters are aligned with the provisions set forth in the applicable legislation. The Government highlights that the introduction of Article 20 of Decree-Law 76/2020 has resulted in improvements to pension arrangements for all personnel, including those retired on or after January 1, 2020, effectively addressing disparities in retirement benefits. It maintains that no unjust conditions exist for retired firefighters, as measures were taken to ensure that all retired firefighters receive equitable treatment in their pension provisions. 93. The Government further argues that the allegations of unequal treatment regarding pensions are unfounded, as the legislative measures aim to create equitable social security provisions for all firefighters, regardless of their hiring date. The Government asserts that the reforms undertaken effectively enhance the social security framework for firefighters.  B – Assessment of the Committee 94. The Committee recalls that under Article 12§3 of the Charter, States Parties undertake to endeavour to raise progressively the system of social security to a higher level. When reforms restrict the social security system, they must be justified, including with respect to sustainability, and must maintain at least a basic compulsory and sufficiently extensive social security system. 95. In respect of Article 12§3, the Committee assesses whether the obligation to endeavour to raise progressively the system of social security to a higher level has been complied with. The Committee recalls that in this sense, Article 12§3 lays down an obligation to regularly adjust social security benefit rates in order to compensate for any rise in the cost of living (indexation). The temporary decrease, limited increase, or the absence of increase in the real values of some social security benefits over a given period is not necessarily incompatible with Article 12§3 (Finnish Society of Social Rights v. Finland, Complaint No.88/2012, decision on the merits of 9 September 2014, §88). 96. The Committee recalls that under Article 12§3 the examples of measures taken to raise the system of social security at a higher level include the expansion of schemes, protection against new risks, or an increase in the level of benefits (Statement of interpretation on Article 12, Conclusions XVI-1, Conclusions 2013 Georgia). 97. The Committee further recalls that under Article 12§3, in line with its case law, it also assesses reforms of the social security system. States Parties may consider that consolidating public finances, in order to prevent deficits and debt interest from increasing, is one way of safeguarding the social security system. In particular, they may consider that adopting measures to ensure that old-age pension schemes are adequately funded in the light of demographic changes is part of this process (Conclusions XIV-1 (1998), Italy). 98. The Committee further recalls that the obligation under Article 12§3 to progressively raise the system of social security to a higher level will be assessed over a period of time and take into account both the current level of the system and the economic situation in the country concerned (Sindacato autonomo Pensionati Or.S.A. v. Italy, Complaint No. 167/2018, decision on the merits of 7 December 2022, §47). The expansion of schemes, protection against new risks, or an increase(s) in the level of benefits are all examples of improvement (Conclusions 2013, Georgia). Compliance with Article 12§3 requires that the existing social security systems be adapted to the specific situation and needs of the workers concerned, in order to guarantee that they enjoy the social benefits included within the scope of Article 12§1 (Conclusions 2021, Lithuania). 99. In the present case, the Committee notes that there are two groups of firefighters in relation to whom CO.NA.PO. alleges discriminatory treatment in pension entitlements. The first group comprises those recruited before 1 January 2020, who remain in active service and whose pension entitlements for the period 2010-2020 do not reflect the salary equalisation introduced later. The second group includes those who retired before 1 January 2020 (approximately 350 firefighters) and therefore did not benefit from the salary alignment adopted through Decree-Law No.76/2020. 100. With regard to the first group, CO.NA.PO. submits that the lack of adjustment of salaries and risk allowances during 2010-2020 resulted in a reduced contribution base and, consequently, a future pension disadvantage compared to their colleagues recruited after 2020. It contends that this situation constitutes a structural shortcoming affecting a specific category of workers. The Committee observes in this respect that although firefighters recruited before 2020 did not benefit from the higher salary levels during the 2010-2020 period, the reform introduced as of 1 January 2020 was gradually applied uniformly to all firefighters then in active service. 101. The Committee considers that the absence of retroactive compensation affects the contribution base for the pre-2020 period and will therefore result in less favourable future pension entitlements for this group. However, the Committee considers that the prospective application of the reform ensured equal treatment from the date of its entry into force. While it did not remedy past disparities, this situation does not amount to a failure to progressively raise the level of protection as the Charter does not impose an obligation to redress past disparities in pension contribution bases once a harmonised regime is introduced. 102. Regarding the second group of firefighters who retired before 1 January 2020, CO.NA.PO. argues that the failure to implement the salary reform envisaged by Law No.183/2010 until 2020 unjustly excluded these individuals from its beneficial effects on pension calculations. CO.NA.PO. asks the Government to introduce positive legislative measures for the benefit of the entire category of workers concerned, and in particular by means of transitional measures to equalise retirement pensions and compensate those who retired before 2020, for the damage caused by the differences not compensated for. The Committee takes note of the concerns raised but reiterates that the Charter does not require the retroactive extension of improved social security provisions to individuals who retired prior to their final adoption, and the absence of such retroactive measures does not, in itself, constitute a failure to progressively enhance the level of protection as required under Article 12§3. 103. The Committee recalls that under Article 12, States enjoy a wide margin of discretion as to how they organise their social security systems, including by defining the personal scope of schemes providing health care benefits, as long as a significant percentage of population is covered and the benefits provided are sufficiently extensive (Sindicato dos Magistrados do Ministério Público (SMMP) v. Portugal, Complaint No. 43/2007, decision on the merits of 3 December 2008, §44). 104. The Committee further recalls that it falls on the State Party to maintain the social security system on a satisfactory level that takes into account the legitimate expectations of beneficiaries of the system and the right of all persons to effective enjoyment of the right to social security (Federation of employed pensioners of Greece (IKA-ETAM) v. Greece, Complaint No. 76/2012, decision on the merits of 7 December 2012, §69). The Committee has considered that as has been done by the European Court of Human Rights as concerns the Convention, any decisions made in respect of pension entitlements must respect the need to reconcile the general interest with individual rights, including any legitimate expectations that individuals may have in respect of the stability of the rules applicable to social security benefits (IKA-ETAM v. Greece, op.cit., §82). 105. As regards the instant case, the Committee considers, that the absence of compensatory measures for the contribution period of 2010-2020, intended to ensure pension levels comparable to those of firefighters recruited after 2020 cannot as such be regarded as a restrictive evolution in the social security system in relation to old-age benefits, in the meaning of Article 12§3. It rather reflects a legislative decision not to extend the improved pension regime introduced as of a specific date to persons who retired before that date or who, while still in employment, had been subject to the old regime during that period. 106. The Committee recognises that firefighters who retired prior to 1 January 2020 may have had legitimate expectations arising from the legislative objectives of Law No. 183/2010. However, the failure to retroactively guarantee a higher pension or to provide compensation to a specific category of workers for a specific past period under the old pension regime, does not represent a restrictive evolution of the system, nor does it deprive these workers of the protection they have under social security system at a satisfactory level. The improvement of a pension regime from a specific date does not guarantee a right to the new, more favourable pension conditions for the persons who retired prior to that date or who, while still in service, accrued pension rights under the previous regime. Therefore, the Committee considers that there is no violation of Article 12§3 taken in respect of the two groups of firefighters described above. 107. As regards the alleged violation of Article E read in conjunction with Article 12§3, the Committee refers to its findings under Article 1§2 (see §§ 75 and 79). It considers that the differential treatment resulting from the absence of retroactive salary equalisation for firefighters who retired before 2020, as well as those still in active service during the 2010-2020 period does not amount to discrimination within the meaning of the Charter. 108. For these reasons, the Committee holds that there is no violation of Article 12§3 alone or Article E read in conjunction with 12§3 of the Charter. CONCLUSION For these reasons, the Committee concludes: - by 14 votes to 1 that there is no violation of Article 1§2 of the Charter; - by 14 votes to 1 that there is no violation of Article 12§3 alone or Article E read in conjunction with 12§3 of the Charter. Tatiana PUIU Rapporteur Aoife NOLAN President Henrik KRISTENSEN Executive Secretary In accordance with Rule 35§1 of the Rules of the Committee, a separate dissenting opinion of Carmen SALCEDO BELTRÁN is appended to this decision. SEPARATE DISSENTING OPINION OF CARMEN SALCEDO BELTRÁN 1. I voted against the Committee’s majority decision, which found that there had been no violation of Article 1§2 and no violation of Article 12§3 read alone or Article E read in conjunction with Article 12§3 of the European Social Charter. 2. The Committee noted from the complaint that the situation of the national firefighters corps was discriminatory. There is no doubt as to the unequal treatment to which they were subjected with regard to remuneration and supplementary allowances. This state of affairs is legally established and was implicitly recognised by the Government when it adopted Law No. 183/2010, which aimed to reform labour market regulations and employment conditions and came into force on 24 November 2010. 3. However, it was not until 10 years later, with the approval of Decree-Law No. 76 of 16 July 2020, that the Government addressed this discriminatory practice. Such a delay cannot be disregarded, particularly as the text did not introduce any transitional measures to remedy the prejudice suffered during that extended period. The Government is the only party responsible for the excessive delays, and it must bear the consequences of any resulting damage. 4. The legal framework discriminated against a specific group. The legislator admitted as much when the reform was adopted in an attempt to resolve this, but ten years passed before the situation was actually rectified. Furthermore, in merely remedying the situation for the future and failing to make retroactive reparations, the legislator took absolutely no account of the harm caused by the delay. 5. The majority of the Committee considered that the delay fell within the Government’s margin of appreciation. At the same time, it found that the absence of retroactive salary equalisation for firefighters who retired before 2020, as well as those still in active service during the 2010-2020 period, did not amount to discrimination within the meaning of the Charter. 6. For my part, I consider and wish to emphasise that this is a violation of the Charter with a long-term effect, and that, consequently, a correction without transitional measures would constitute an ongoing violation of the rights enshrined in the Charter. The Committee should have applied its established case law on jurisdiction ratione temporis (Marangopoulos Foundation for Human Rights v. Greece, Complaint No. 30/2005, decision on admissibility of 10 October 2005, § 15) in line with that of the European Court of Human Rights in Blečić v. Croatia (application no. 59532/00, judgment of 8 March 2006, Grand Chamber, §§63-76) and Šilih v. Slovenia (application no. 71463/01, judgment of 9 April 2009, Grand Chamber, § 146), which were both taken into account in the case of the Centre on Housing Rights and Evictions (COHRE), v. Croatia (Complaint No. 52/2008, decision on the merits of 22 June 2010, §§22-26). When assessing whether there has been a violation, the decisive factor is “the exact time of the alleged interference”, i.e. the effects produced by the facts constituting the violation. In the present case, the discriminatory situation persists and will continue to produce effects in the long term, as the group concerned will endure the effects of the discrimination in their salary and pension calculations for many years. 7. I would also like to express my disagreement with the Committee’s broad interpretation of the margin of appreciation afforded to States in relation to the time limit within which they must remedy a situation of discrimination. Certainly, States should be afforded a margin of appreciation in how they apply rights in certain situations. 8. However, the framework for this margin of appreciation must always be defined in a consistent manner and take due account of the legitimate interests involved (S.H. and Others v. Austria, application no. 57813/00, judgment of 3 November 2011, § 100). I do not concur with the Committee’s view that the State has infinite temporal discretion in addressing discriminatory circumstances. This interpretation may be regarded as akin to validating or even overlooking situations in which States only address the issue years or even decades later, having regard only to the actual moment of compliance and taking no account of the detrimental effects experienced by citizens throughout the duration of the infringement. 9. Compliance with the obligations arising from the Charter, and where relevant, from the Committee’s case law, cannot be left to the States’ discretion or incorporated into their margin of appreciation. The rectification of a violation must necessarily be accompanied by compensation for the harm suffered by those affected by the act in question. Otherwise, as in the present case, discrimination is perpetuated. 10. The aim and purpose of the Charter, being a human rights protection instrument, is to protect rights not merely theoretically, but also in fact (International Commission of Jurists (ICJ) v. Portugal, Complaint No. 1/1998, decision on the merits of 9 September 1999, § 32). In the instant case, the 10-year delay in rectifying the discriminatory practices is both unjustified and especially long. The absence of transitional measures not only exacerbated the damage suffered by the affected group, but also undermined the effectiveness of the guaranteed right. The discrimination has not been rectified; it persists and will continue to have an impact on those affected. 11. For all these reasons, I consider that the Committee should have found a violation of Article 1§2 and Article 12§3 read alone or Article E read in conjunction with Article 12§3 of the Charter.
